title: Lipstick on Polyester
stainChemistry: Lipstick is a four-component stain: pigment (iron oxides, lakes), oil (castor oil, lanolin), wax (beeswax, carnauba), and dye (D&C reds). On polyester, the wax creates a hydrophobic shell around the pigment, the oil penetrates the fiber, and the dye transfers color. Treatment must break through the wax barrier to access the underlying components.
whyThisWorks: On polyester, the treatment addresses components in order: first dissolve the wax/oil matrix with POG or solvent, then lift the pigment with surfactant, then address any remaining dye with oxidizer. Scraping excess first prevents spreading. Working from the back of the fabric (when possible) pushes the stain out rather than deeper in.
defaultAssumption: Assume stain age is unknown and possibly heat-set unless user specifies otherwise. Start conservative.

steps: 1. Apply POG to dissolve the oil/wax component — 5-10 min dwell time
2. Apply NSD to lift dissolved residue and pigment — 3-5 min dwell time
3. If color remains: apply H2O2 6% on colorfast fabrics — 5-10 min dwell time, monitor
4. Apply Acetic Acid 28% (diluted 1:10) as neutralizing rinse — 1-2 min
1. Scrape or blot excess — do NOT rub (spreading worsens it)
2. Apply Dawn dish soap directly to the stain. Work gently — 5-10 min dwell time
3. Blot with clean white cloth. Repeat Dawn application until stain fades
4. Rinse with warm water (unless protein component present — then cold)
diyProtocol.whenToStop: Stop if the stain hasn't improved after 3 rounds of treatment. Combo stains are inherently harder — a professional cleaner has specialized chemistry for each component
warnings: Treat each component in order — don't skip steps
Cold water always — heat sets protein components permanently

neverDo: No strong alkali at high temperature
fiberSensitivities: Oleophilic — grabs and holds oil stains tenaciously
Retains odors due to lipophilic surface
Most chemicals are safe on polyester

escalation.when: If lipstick stain persists after 2-3 professional treatment cycles, or if the stain has been heat-set, or if home treatment has not improved the stain after 3 attempts
escalation.whatToTell: Explain that the stain is lipstick on polyester, how old it is, and what products were already used. This helps the professional choose the right chemistry without duplicating failed approaches

contextQuestions: Has it been exposed to heat (dryer, iron, hot water)?
Have you already tried treating it? If so, with what products?
Can you identify the specific components?
Is there a greasy/oily feel to the stain?
deepSolveHooks.modifierNotes: Stain age and heat exposure are the two biggest factors. Fresh stains with no heat exposure have the highest success rate. Heat-set or aged stains may require professional treatment.
